per_question.input: To which supervisory authority should I report?
per_question.output:
To determine the appropriate **Supervisory Authority** to report to, your query should be framed within the context of **data protection regulations**, particularly the **General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)**. The relevant supervisory authority depends on your role (e.g., whether you are a **Controller**, **Processor**, or **Data Subject**) and the jurisdiction where the processing of personal data occurs. Below is a structured guide to help identify the correct authority:

---

### **1. For Data Subjects (Individuals)**
If you are a **data subject** (an individual whose personal data is being processed) and believe your rights under the GDPR have been infringed, you should lodge a complaint with the **Supervisory Authority** of the **Member State where you reside**, where you work, or where the alleged infringement occurred. This is based on the following provisions:
- **Article 77(1) of the GDPR** states:
  > *"Without prejudice to any other administrative or judicial remedy, every data subject shall have the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority, in particular in the Member State of his or her habitual residence, place of work or place of the alleged infringement..."*
- The supervisory authority will then **investigate the complaint** and inform you of the progress and outcome.

---

### **2. For Controllers and Processors**
If you are a **Controller** (entity determining the purposes and means of processing personal data) or a **Processor** (entity processing data on behalf of a Controller), the relevant **Supervisory Authority** depends on the location of your **main establishment** or the **establishment where the processing takes place**:

#### **A. Lead Supervisory Authority (Cross-Border Processing)**
- The **Lead Supervisory Authority** is responsible for overseeing **cross-border processing** (processing activities that occur in more than one Member State).
- The **Lead Supervisory Authority** is the supervisory authority of the **Member State where the Controller or Processor has its main establishment**.
  - This is explicitly outlined in **Article 56(1) of the GDPR**:
    > *"Without prejudice to Article 55, the supervisory authority of the main establishment or of the single establishment of the controller or processor shall be competent to act as lead supervisory authority for the cross-border processing carried out by that controller or processor..."*
- The **Lead Supervisory Authority** acts as the **sole interlocutor** for the Controller or Processor in cross-border processing cases.

#### **B. Local Supervisory Authority (Processing in One Member State)**
- If your processing activities are **limited to a single Member State**, you should report to the **local supervisory authority** of that Member State.
- **Article 55(1) of the GDPR** states:
  > *"Each supervisory authority shall be competent for the performance of the tasks assigned to and the exercise of the powers conferred on it in accordance with this Regulation on the territory of its own Member State."*
- If the matter is purely local (e.g., processing only affects individuals in one Member State), the **lead supervisory authority mechanism does not apply**.

---

### **3. For Specific Cases**
#### **A. Public Authorities or Bodies**
- If you are a **public authority or body** (e.g., government agency, tax authority), the **Supervisory Authority** of the **Member State where you are established** will oversee your compliance.
- **Article 55(2) of the GDPR** specifies:
  > *"Where processing is carried out by public authorities or private bodies acting on the basis of point (c) or (e) of Article 6(1), the supervisory authority of the Member State concerned shall be competent."*

#### **B. Courts in Judicial Capacity**
- **Supervisory Authorities do not have competence over courts acting in their judicial capacity**.
- **Article 55(3) of the GDPR** states:
  > *"Supervisory authorities shall not be competent to supervise processing operations of courts acting in their judicial capacity."*

---
### **4. How to Identify Your Supervisory Authority**
To find the **Supervisory Authority** responsible for your case:
1. **For Data Subjects**: Identify the **Member State where you reside**, work, or where the alleged infringement occurred.
   - Example: If you live in France and your data was processed by a company in Germany, you should report to the **French Supervisory Authority (CNIL)**.
2. **For Controllers/Processors**: Identify your **main establishment** or the **Member State where the processing occurs**.
   - Example: If your company is based in Ireland and processes data of EU residents, the **Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC)** is the Lead Supervisory Authority.

---
### **5. List of National Supervisory Authorities**
Below is a list of **Supervisory Authorities** in key EU Member States. You can find a full list on the [European Data Protection Board (EDPB) website](https://edpb.europa.eu/about-edpb/board/members_en):

| **Member State**       | **Supervisory Authority**                     | **Website**                                      |
|------------------------|-----------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| Austria               | Österreichische Datenschutzbehörde             | [www.dsb.gv.at](https://www.dsb.gv.at)         |
| Belgium               | Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens                   | [www.dataprotectionauthority.be](https://www.dataprotectionauthority.be) |
| Bulgaria              | Комисия за защита на личните данни             | [www.cpdp.bg](https://www.cpdp.bg)             |
| Croatia               | Azur za zaštitu osobnih podataka               | [www.azop.hr](https://www.azop.hr)             |
| Cyprus                | Επιτροπή Προστασίας Δεδομένων Προσωπικού Χαρακτήρα | [www.dataprotection.gov.cy](https://www.dataprotection.gov.cy) |
| Czech Republic        | Úřad pro ochranu osobních údajů                | [www.uoou.cz](https://www.uoou.cz)             |
| Denmark               | Datatilsynet                                 | [www.datatilsynet.dk](https://www.datatilsynet.dk) |
| Estonia               | Andmekaitseinspektsioon                       | [www.aki.ee](https://www.aki.ee)               |
| Finland               | Tietosuojavaltuutettu                        | [www.tietosuoja.fi](https://www.tietosuoja.fi) |
| France                | Commission nationale de l'informatique et des libertés (CNIL) | [www.cnil.fr](https://www.cnil.fr) |
| Germany               | Der Bundesbeauftragte für den Datenschutz und die Informationsfreiheit (BfDI) | [www.bfdi.bund.de](https://www.bfdi.bund.de) |
| Greece                | Αρχή Προστασίας Δεδομένων Προσωπικού Χαρακτήρα  | [www.dpa.gr](https://www.dpa.gr)               |

| Italy                 | Garante per la protezione dei dati personali   | [www.garanteprivacy.it](https://www.garanteprivacy.it) |
| Latvia                | Datu valsts inspekcija                       | [www.dvi.gov.lv](https://www.dvi.gov.lv)       |

| Luxembourg            | Commission nationale pour la protection des données (CNPD) | [www.cnpd.lu](https://www.cnpd.lu) |
| Malta                 | Information and Data Protection Commissioner    | [www.idpc.org.mt](https://www.idpc.org.mt)      |
| Netherlands           | Autoriteit Persoonsgegevens                    | [www.autoriteitpersoonsgegevens.nl](https://www.autoriteitpersoonsgegevens.nl) |
| Poland                | Urząd Ochrony Danych Osobowych                 | [www.uodo.gov.pl](https://www.uodo.gov.pl)       |
| Portugal              | Comissão Nacional de Proteção de Dados          | [www.cnpd.pt](https://www.cnpd.pt)             |
| Romania               | Autoritatea Națională de Supraveghere a Prelucrării Datelor cu Caracter Personal | [www.dataprotection.ro](https://www.dataprotection.ro) |
| Slovakia              | Úrad pre ochranu osobných údajov               | [www.uoou.sk](https://www.uoou.sk)             |
| Slovenia              | Informacijski pooblaščenec                    | [www.ip-rs.si](https://www.ip-rs.si)           |
| Spain                 | Agencia Española de Protección de Datos (AEPD)  | [www.aepd.es](https://www.aepd.es)             |
| Sweden                | Integritetsskyddsmyndigheten                   | [www.imy.se](https://www.imy.se)               |

---
### **Summary of Steps**
1. **Determine your role**:
   - Are you a **Data Subject**, **Controller**, or **Processor**?
2. **Identify the jurisdiction**:
   - Where do you reside, work, or where is the processing occurring?
3. **Check for cross-border processing**:
   - If processing occurs in multiple Member States, identify the **Lead Supervisory Authority**.
4. **Visit the website of the relevant Supervisory Authority** to file a complaint or seek guidance.

---
### **References**
- [1] *General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Article 55*
- [2] *General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Article 56*
- [3] *General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), Article 77*
per_question.ground_truth:
The GDPR establishes the European Data Protection Board (EDPB) (not EDPS, which is a separate EU institution for supervising EU bodies) to ensure consistent application of the GDPR across the EU. It provides guidance and helps resolve disputes between national supervisory authorities.

For companies with establishments in multiple EU countries, the Lead Supervisory Authority (LSA) of the main establishment acts as a one-stop shop for enforcement. This mechanism simplifies compliance for EU-based multinational companies but does not apply to non-EU companies, even if they have an EU representative.

Data subjects can still file complaints with the authority in their residence, workplace, or where the violation occurred.

In cross-border cases, the LSA coordinates with other concerned authorities, with joint operations and mutual assistance mechanisms in place. In disputes between authorities, the EDPB can issue binding decisions to resolve conflicts and ensure uniform enforcement.

per_question.input: Does the GDPR provide full harmonisation or are there differences between member states?
per_question.output:
The **General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)** (Regulation (EU) 2016/679) establishes a **framework for harmonised data protection rules** across the European Union (EU). However, it does **not provide full harmonisation**, as it includes provisions that allow **Member States to introduce specific rules** to adapt its application or address national particularities. Below is a detailed explanation of the harmonisation level and where differences between Member States may occur:

---

### **1. Areas of Full Harmonisation**
The GDPR introduces a **high degree of harmonisation** by setting out:
- **Uniform legal principles** for the lawful processing of personal data across all Member States.
- **Equivalent protection** for data subjects, regardless of their location within the EU, ensuring a **consistent level of rights** (e.g., right to access, rectification, erasure, and data portability).
- **Standardised obligations** for controllers and processors, such as:
  - Requirements for transparency and fairness in processing.
  - Data minimisation and storage limitation principles.
  - Security measures to protect personal data.
  - Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) for high-risk processing activities.
- **Common enforcement mechanisms**, including the powers of supervisory authorities and administrative fines for infringements.

This harmonisation is designed to **facilitate the free flow of personal data** within the EU while ensuring a **high level of protection** for individuals.

---

### **2. Areas Allowing National Differences**
While the GDPR promotes harmonisation, it also contains **provisions that permit differences between Member States**, particularly in the following areas:

#### **A. Specific Processing Contexts**
The GDPR permits Member States to introduce **additional rules or safeguards** in specific contexts, such as:
- **Processing of personal data for employment purposes**: Member States may lay down **more specific rules** to reconcile the right to data protection with the rights of workers (Article 88). This allows for variations in national labour laws and collective bargaining agreements.
- **Public interest and official authority**: Member States can specify **more detailed rules** for processing activities carried out by public authorities or in the exercise of official authority (Article 6(1)(e)).
- **Health and social care**: Member States may introduce **more granular rules** for processing health data, including additional safeguards and conditions (Article 9(4)).
- **Freedom of expression and information**: Member States must **reconcile** the right to data protection with the freedom of the press and freedom of expression (Article 85). This may lead to variations in how media organisations handle personal data.
- **Research and historical archives**: Member States can introduce **derogations** from certain rights (e.g., right to erasure) for processing activities conducted for scientific, historical, or statistical research purposes (Article 89).

#### **B. Age of Digital Consent**
- The GDPR sets the **minimum age for consent** to data processing at **16 years**, but Member States are allowed to **lower this age** to as young as **13 years** (Article 8).

#### **C. Data Subject Rights and Restrictions**
- Member States may **restrict certain data subject rights** (e.g., the right to object, the right to erasure, or the right to data portability) in specific circumstances, such as:
  - Processing for **public interest**, public health, or statistical purposes.
  - Compliance with **legal obligations** or **exercise of official authority**.
  - **National security or criminal law enforcement** (Article 23).

#### **D. Data Protection by Design and Default**
- While the GDPR requires data protection by design and default (Article 25), Member States may **specify further technical or organisational measures** to ensure compliance.

#### **E. Administrative Fines and Penalties**
- The GDPR sets **maximum administrative fines** (up to **4% of global annual turnover** or €20 million, whichever is higher) for infringements.
- However, Member States may **adopt additional penalties** for infringements not covered by administrative fines, ensuring they are **effective, proportionate, and dissuasive** (Article 84).

#### **F. Supervisory Authorities**
- Member States are responsible for **designating and resourcing** their supervisory authorities, which may lead to **differences in enforcement priorities** or resources.
- The powers and tasks of supervisory authorities are harmonised, but Member States may **specify additional procedures** for handling complaints or conducting investigations.

#### **G. Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs)**
- While the GDPR recognises BCRs as a mechanism for transferring data within corporate groups, Member States may **introduce additional requirements** for their approval or implementation.

---

### **3. Areas Where Harmonisation is Achieved**
The GDPR achieves harmonisation in the following key areas:
- **Definitions**: Uniform definitions for key terms like *personal data*, *processing*, *controller*, *processor*, and *profiling*.
- **Legal bases for processing**: Standardised conditions for lawful processing, including consent, contractual necessity, legal obligation, and legitimate interests.
- **Rights of data subjects**: Harmonised rights such as access, rectification, erasure, portability, and objection.
- **Data breach notification**: Mandatory notification of data breaches to supervisory authorities within **72 hours**.
- **International data transfers**: Common rules for transferring personal data to third countries or international organisations, including mechanisms like Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) and adequacy decisions.
- **Accountability and governance**: Requirements for controllers and processors to demonstrate compliance, including records of processing activities, DPIAs, and data protection impact assessments.

---
### **4. Why Differences Exist**
The **fragmentation** of data protection rules across Member States stems from:
1. **Historical and Legal Traditions**: Differences in national legal systems, cultural norms, and prior data protection laws.
2. **Sector-Specific Regulations**: Existing national laws in areas like employment, healthcare, or media may require adjustments to align with the GDPR.
3. **Public Interest Considerations**: Member States may prioritise different public interest objectives (e.g., national security, law enforcement, or public health) that justify additional rules.
4. **Technological and Economic Factors**: Variations in the adoption of new technologies or economic conditions may influence how Member States implement the GDPR.

---
### **5. Impact of Differences**
While these differences can create **legal uncertainty** for businesses operating across borders, they allow Member States to:
- **Address national priorities** (e.g., protecting workers' rights or ensuring public health).
- **Enhance flexibility** for certain processing activities while maintaining a baseline of protection.
- **Adapt to local contexts**, such as differences in administrative procedures or enforcement practices.

However, the EU has repeatedly emphasised the need to **reduce fragmentation** to ensure a **level playing field** and **legal certainty**. The GDPR itself includes mechanisms to promote consistency, such as:
- The **European Data Protection Board (EDPB)**, which issues guidelines and binding decisions to ensure harmonised application.
- **Cooperation and consistency mechanisms** between supervisory authorities.

---
### **References**
- [1] Regulation (EU) 2016/679 of the European Parliament and of the Council on the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data (General Data Protection Regulation)
- [2] Recital (10) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679
- [3] Article 88 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (processing in the context of employment)
- [4] Article 9(4) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (processing of special categories of personal data)
- [5] Article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (age of digital consent)
per_question.ground_truth:
Although the GDPR is directly applicable across all EU Member States, it allows for national variations in over 60 areas. This leads to differences such as:

The age of consent for children (between 13 and 16)

Criteria for appointing a DPO

National rules on employee data processing

Conditions for processing data for scientific, archiving, or employment purposes

Exceptions to data subject rights and special rules for sensitive or criminal data

National supervisory authorities enforce the GDPR, which may result in variations in how data protection is applied. Additionally, Directive 2016/680 (for police and criminal justice data) must be transposed into national law, allowing Member States flexibility in how its goals are implemented.

per_question.input: How do I ensure that my organisation is GDPR-compliant?
per_question.output:
To ensure that your organisation is **GDPR-compliant**, you must adhere to the principles, obligations, and safeguards outlined in **Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR)**. Below is a structured guide to help your organisation achieve and maintain compliance.

---

### **1. Understand the Core GDPR Principles**
The GDPR is built on seven key principles that must guide all data processing activities. Your organisation must ensure:

- **Lawfulness, Fairness, and Transparency**:
  - Processing of personal data must have a **legal basis** (e.g., consent, contract, legal obligation, legitimate interest, or vital interests).
  - Data subjects must be informed about **what data is collected, why it is collected, and how it will be used** in a clear and transparent manner.
  - *Reference: [32]*

- **Purpose Limitation**:
  - Personal data must only be collected for **specified, explicit, and legitimate purposes** and not further processed in a way incompatible with those purposes.
  - *Reference: [32]*

- **Data Minimisation**:
  - Only collect and process **personal data that is adequate, relevant, and necessary** for the stated purposes.
  - Implement **data minimisation by design and by default** in all systems and processes.
  - *Reference: [78]*

- **Accuracy**:
  - Ensure personal data is **accurate and kept up to date**.
  - Rectify or erase inaccurate data without undue delay.
  - *Reference: [5]*

- **Storage Limitation**:
  - Do not retain personal data for **longer than necessary** for the purposes it was collected.
  - Implement **data retention policies** and **automated deletion mechanisms** where possible.
  - *Reference: [5]*

- **Integrity and Confidentiality (Security)**:
  - Personal data must be processed in a manner that ensures **appropriate security**, including protection against unauthorised or unlawful processing, accidental loss, destruction, or damage.
  - Use **technical and organisational measures** such as encryption, access controls, and regular security audits.
  - *Reference: [83]*

- **Accountability**:
  - Organisations must be able to **demonstrate compliance** with all GDPR principles.
  - Maintain **records of processing activities**, policies, and procedures.
  - *Reference: [78]*

---

### **2. Identify Your Role Under GDPR**
Your responsibilities depend on whether your organisation acts as a **Controller**, **Processor**, or both:

- **Data Controller**:
  - Determines **purposes and means** of processing personal data.
  - Must comply with **GDPR Articles 5-40**, including:
    - Implementing a **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** for high-risk processing.
    - Ensuring **data subject rights** (access, rectification, erasure, portability, etc.) are respected.
    - Appointing a **Data Protection Officer (DPO)** if required.
    - Maintaining **records of processing activities** (unless exempt).
    - *Reference: [24], [30]*

- **Data Processor**:
  - Processes data **on behalf of a controller**.
  - Must comply with **GDPR Articles 28-30**, including:
    - Processing data **only under documented instructions** from the controller.
    - Implementing **technical and organisational measures** to ensure security.
    - Recording all processing activities and cooperating with supervisory authorities.
    - *Reference: [28], [29]*

---
### **3. Implement Technical and Organisational Measures**
GDPR requires organisations to implement **appropriate safeguards** to protect personal data. These include:

- **Data Protection by Design and by Default**:
  - Integrate data protection into **system design, development, and business practices**.
  - Ensure **default settings** limit data collection and use to the minimum necessary.
  - *Reference: [78]*

- **Data Security**:
  - Use **encryption**, **access controls**, and **role-based permissions** to secure data.
  - Conduct **regular risk assessments** and **vulnerability testing**.
  - Implement **incident response plans** for data breaches.
  - *Reference: [83]*

- **Pseudonymisation and Anonymisation**:
  - Pseudonymise data where possible to reduce identifiability risks.
  - Anonymise data where no longer needed for its original purpose.
  - *Reference: [78]*

- **Data Protection Policies and Procedures**:
  - Develop and document **internal policies** for data handling, retention, and breach notification.
  - Ensure employees are trained on **GDPR compliance and data protection awareness**.
  - *Reference: [24]*

- **Vendor and Subcontractor Management**:
  - Ensure any **third-party processors or vendors** handling personal data are **GDPR-compliant**.
  - Use **data processing agreements (DPAs)** to govern data sharing.
  - Require processors to demonstrate compliance via **certifications, codes of conduct, or GDPR adequacy decisions**.
  - *Reference: [81]*

---
### **4. Ensure Compliance with Data Subject Rights**
Data subjects have **strong rights under GDPR**. Your organisation must:

- **Provide Transparent Information**:
  - Display a **privacy notice** with clear information about data processing, legal bases, purposes, and data subject rights.
  - *Reference: [32]*

- **Facilitate Data Subject Requests**:
  - Allow data subjects to **access, rectify, or erase their data**.
  - Respond to **data portability requests** by providing data in a **structured, machine-readable format**.
  - Permit **withdrawal of consent** or objections to processing.
  - *Reference: [68] [69]*

- **Honour the Right to be Forgotten (Erasure)**:
  - Delete personal data upon request, unless retention is required by law.
  - *Reference: [68]*

- **Respect Automated Decision-Making Limits**:
  - Provide **human intervention** in decisions solely based on automated processing.
  - *Reference: [71]*

---
### **5. Conduct Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs)**
For **high-risk processing activities**, such as:

- Large-scale processing of **special categories of data** (e.g., health data, biometric data).
- Systematic monitoring of individuals (e.g., profiling).
- Automated decision-making with legal or significant effects.

Your organisation must:
- Conduct a **DPIA** to identify and mitigate risks.
- Consult the **supervisory authority** if risks cannot be sufficiently addressed.
- *Reference: [84]*

---
### **6. Appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO)**
A DPO is required if:

- Your organisation is a **public authority** (except for courts).
- Core activities involve **large-scale processing of special categories of data**.
- Core activities involve **systematic monitoring of individuals on a large scale**.

The DPO:
- Provides **expert advice** on GDPR compliance.
- Acts as a **contact point for supervisory authorities**.
- Monitors **privacy compliance** and trains staff.
- *Reference: [80] [78]*

---
### **7. Prepare for Data Breaches**
- Implement **robust breach detection and notification procedures**.
- Notify the **supervisory authority within 72 hours** of a breach (unless the breach is unlikely to pose a risk).
- Communicate **high-risk breaches to data subjects** without undue delay.
- *Reference: [84]*

---
### **8. Ensure International Data Transfers are Compliant**
- If transferring data outside the **EU/EEA**:
  - Use **standard contractual clauses (SCCs)**.
  - Rely on **adequacy decisions** from the European Commission.
  - Implement **Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs)** for corporate groups.
  - Ensure the recipient country offers **equivalent data protection**.
  - *Reference: [101], [45]*

---
### **9. Monitor and Review Compliance Regularly**
- Perform **periodic audits** of data processing activities.
- Ensure **policies and procedures are kept up to date** with evolving regulations.
- Adapt to **new technologies and risks** through **continuous improvement**.
- *Reference: [78]*

---
### **10. Engage with Supervisory Authorities**
- Register with your **national supervisory authority**, if required.
- Cooperate with them during **audits, investigations, or breach notifications**.
- Participate in **regulatory sandboxes** for innovation and compliance testing.
- *Reference: [83], [139]*

---
### **Quick Checklist for GDPR Compliance**
| **Action**                          | **GDPR Reference** |
|-------------------------------------|--------------------|
| Identify legal basis for data processing | [32]              |
| Implement data protection by design   | [78]              |
| Appoint a Data Protection Officer     | [80]              |
| Conduct DPIAs for high-risk processing| [84]              |
| Create a privacy notice               | [32]              |
| Provide data subject rights          | [68], [69]        |
| Train staff on GDPR                  | [78]              |
| Secure data with encryption/controls  | [83]              |
| Maintain records of processing        | [30]              |
| Ensure third-party processor compliance | [81]              |
| Register with supervisory authority    | [84]              |
| Prepare breach notification procedures  | [84]              |

---
### **References**

- [1] Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (General Data Protection Regulation)
- [2] Regulation (EU) 2016/679 Recitals (80)-(84)
- [3] Article 24, Responsibility of the Controller
- [4] Article 28, Processor Responsibilities
- [5] Article 5, Principles Relating to Processing of Personal Data
- [6] Article 30, Records of Processing Activities
- [7] Article 35, Data Protection Impact Assessment
- [8] Article 37, Appointment of a Data Protection Officer
- [9] Article 44-49, Data Transfers to Third Countries
- [10] Recitals (78)-(85), Data Protection by Design, Security, and Accountability
per_question.ground_truth:
Establishing a clear structure and assigning responsibilities is a critical first step toward GDPR compliance. Early efforts to raise data protection awareness and implement policies and procedures make long-term compliance more manageable.

Operationally, the process begins with:

Assessing current data processing activities

Registering existing processing operations

Conducting a data protection audit, if needed

Identifying compliance gaps and associated risks

Prioritizing corrective actions

GDPR compliance is a continuous process. New processing activities must be assessed in advance, and existing policies, procedures, and security measures must be regularly reviewed and updated. Ongoing staff training and documentation of changes are essential to maintaining compliance.

per_question.input: What is the risk of my organisation to comply with data protection rules as set by the GDPR?
per_question.output:
To assess the risk of your organisation to comply with **General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR)** rules, it is essential to evaluate both **external** and **internal** factors that may impact compliance. Below is a structured analysis based on the **GDPR** and related frameworks provided in the context.

---

### **Key Risk Factors for GDPR Compliance**

#### **1. Nature of Processing Activities**
The GDPR classifies processing activities based on their **risk level** to the rights and freedoms of natural persons. Your organisation’s risk exposure depends on:
- **Types of personal data processed**:
  - **General personal data** (e.g., names, contact details) pose a lower risk.
  - **Special categories of personal data** (e.g., health data, biometric data, genetic data, racial/ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, trade union membership, sexual orientation, or data related to criminal convictions) are **high-risk** by default and subject to stricter rules under **Article 9**.
  - **Profiling and automated decision-making**: If your organisation uses AI or profiling techniques to evaluate aspects of individuals (e.g., credit scoring, recruitment, or health assessments), this may trigger a **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** due to the risk of discrimination, bias, or privacy violations.
- **Scale of processing**:
  - Large-scale processing of personal data (e.g., processing data of a significant number of individuals) increases the risk of **non-compliance** and potential fines.
  - Processing involving **systematic monitoring of individuals** (e.g., tracking employee activity, tracking website users) also poses higher risks.

- **Purpose and context**:
  - Processing for **scientific research, public interest, or archiving** may have **derogations**, but these must be carefully justified.
  - Processing for **marketing, advertising, or profiling** requires explicit consent and strict adherence to transparency obligations.

---

#### **2. Legal Bases for Processing**
Your organisation must ensure that all processing activities comply with **Article 6** of the GDPR, which requires a **lawful basis** for processing. Common legal bases include:
- **Consent**: Must be **freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous** (Article 7). Withdrawal of consent must be as easy as giving it.
- **Contractual necessity**: Processing is necessary for fulfilling a contract (e.g., processing employee data for payroll).
- **Legal obligation**: Processing is required by law (e.g., tax reporting, employment law compliance).
- **Legitimate interests**: Must be balanced against the rights and freedoms of the data subject. This basis is **not applicable** for special categories of data or high-risk processing.
- **Public interest or official authority**: Processing is necessary for tasks carried out in the public interest.

**Risk**: Using an **incorrect legal basis** for processing can lead to **violations of data subject rights**, complaints, and fines.

---

#### **3. Data Subject Rights**
The GDPR grants data subjects **strong rights**, which your organisation must facilitate:
- **Right to access**: Data subjects can request access to their data.
- **Right to rectification**: Data subjects can request corrections to inaccurate data.
- **Right to erasure (Right to be Forgotten)**: Data subjects can request deletion of their data under specific conditions.
- **Right to restriction of processing**: Data subjects can request that processing be paused.
- **Right to data portability**: Data subjects can request their data in a machine-readable format and transfer it to another controller.
- **Right to object**: Data subjects can object to processing based on legitimate interests or direct marketing.
- **Right not to be subject to automated decision-making**: Includes profiling that produces legal or similarly significant effects.

**Risk**: Failure to **respond to data subject requests** within **one month** (as per Article 12) can result in complaints to supervisory authorities and fines.

---

#### **4. Data Security and Breach Management**
The GDPR requires organisations to implement **appropriate technical and organisational measures (TOMs)** to ensure data security. Key risks include:
- **Data breaches**: Unauthorised access, loss, or disclosure of personal data. Organisations must report breaches to **supervisory authorities within 72 hours** (Article 33) and, in some cases, inform affected data subjects (Article 34).
- **Insufficient security measures**: Weak encryption, lack of access controls, or inadequate staff training can lead to breaches.
- **Poor vendor management**: If your organisation uses **third-party processors** (e.g., cloud providers, payroll services), they must also comply with GDPR. Controllers must ensure processors have **sufficient guarantees** (Article 28).

**Risk**: A **data breach** can result in:
  - Supervisory authority investigations.
  - Fines up to **€10 million or 2% of global annual turnover**, whichever is higher (Article 83).
  - Reputational damage and loss of customer trust.

---

#### **5. Data Transfers Outside the EU/EEA**
If your organisation transfers personal data to **third countries or international organisations**, GDPR imposes strict conditions (Chapter V):
- Transfers require an **adequacy decision** from the EU (e.g., transfers to Canada under the **EU-US Data Privacy Framework**).
- If no adequacy decision exists, organisations must use **standard contractual clauses (SCCs)**, **binding corporate rules (BCRs)**, or other safeguards.
- Transfers to **high-risk countries** (e.g., those without adequate data protection laws) are subject to additional scrutiny.

**Risk**: Non-compliant transfers can result in fines and **invalid contracts**.

---

#### **6. Data Protection by Design and Default**
GDPR requires organisations to implement **data protection by design** (integrating privacy into system development) and **by default** (ensuring only necessary data is collected and processed). Failure to do so can lead to:
- **Problems with system design**: Systems that do not support privacy (e.g., excessive data collection, lack of encryption) are non-compliant.
- **Inability to demonstrate compliance**: Without documentation and policies, organisations may struggle to show regulators they meet GDPR requirements.

**Risk**: Non-compliance can result in **fines and reputational damage**.

---

#### **7. Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs)**
If your organisation engages in **high-risk processing** (Article 35), it must conduct a **DPIA** to assess and mitigate risks to data subjects. High-risk processing includes:
- **Systematic and extensive profiling** (e.g., credit scoring, employee monitoring).
- **Large-scale processing of special categories of data** (e.g., health data).
- **Use of AI for biometric identification** (e.g., facial recognition).

**Risk**:
- Failure to conduct a **DPIA** for high-risk processing can result in **fines**.
- Supervisory authorities may **ban processing operations** if risks cannot be mitigated.

---
#### **8. Record-Keeping and Accountability**
Organisations must **document compliance** activities, including:
- **Records of processing activities** (Article 30): Required for organisations with more than 250 employees or processing high-risk data.
- **Policies and procedures**: Including Privacy Policies, Data Retention Policies, and Incident Response Plans.
- **Staff training**: Regular data protection training for employees (Article 39).

**Risk**: Lack of documentation can be seen as **evidence of non-compliance**, leading to fines during investigations.

---
#### **9. Supervisory Authority Scrutiny**
If your organisation is subject to a **complaint or investigation** by a supervisory authority (e.g., the **Irish Data Protection Commission** or your local DPA), risks include:
- **Formal warnings or reprimands**.
- **Bans on processing operations**.
- **Administrative fines**:
  - Up to **€10 million or 2% of global annual turnover** for less severe infringements (e.g., failure to implement TOMs or record processing activities).
  - Up to **€20 million or 4% of global annual turnover** for severe infringements (e.g., breaches of data subject rights, unlawful processing of special categories, or non-compliance with international transfers).

---
#### **10. Sector-Specific Risks**
Different sectors face unique GDPR compliance risks:
- **Healthcare**: High risk due to processing of **health data** (special category data under Article 9). Non-compliance can lead to serious privacy violations.
- **Finance**: High risk due to processing **financial data**, fraud detection, and credit scoring.
- **Technology/AI**: High risk due to **automated decision-making, profiling, and AI systems** that may infringe on data subjects' rights.
- **Public sector**: High risk due to **processing large-scale data** and the potential for misuse.

---

### **Steps to Assess and Mitigate Compliance Risks**
1. **Conduct a Data Audit**:
   - Map all processing activities.
   - Identify the types of personal data processed.
   - Document legal bases for processing.
   - Review third-party processors and ensure GDPR-compliant contracts are in place (Article 28).

2. **Implement Data Protection Measures**:
   - Adopt **technical and organisational measures** (e.g., encryption, access controls, pseudonymisation).
   - Ensure **data minimisation** (collect only what is necessary).
   - Implement **privacy by design and default**.

3. **Update Policies and Procedures**:
   - Create or update **Privacy Policies, Cookie Consent Policies, Data Retention Policies, Incident Response Plans, and Staff Training Programmes**.

4. **Conduct a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)**:
   - If your organisation processes **high-risk data** or engages in **automated decision-making**, conduct a DPIA to identify and mitigate risks.

5. **Ensure International Transfer Compliance**:
   - Review data flows to third countries and ensure compliance with **Chapter V of the GDPR** (e.g., SCCs, adequacy decisions, or BCRs).

6. **Train Employees**:
   - Train staff on **GDPR principles, data subject rights, breach reporting, and incident response**.

7. **Monitor and Review Compliance**:
   - Regularly **monitor and review compliance** with GDPR.
   - Stay updated with **guidance from supervisory authorities** and **regulatory changes**.

---
### **Key Takeaways**
- The **risk of non-compliance** depends on the **type of data processed, scale, purpose, and security measures**.
- **Special categories of data and automated decision-making** pose the highest risks.
- **Failure to comply** can result in **fines, reputational damage, loss of customer trust, and potential bans on processing**.
- **DPIAs, record-keeping, employee training, and third-party due diligence** are critical for mitigating risks.

---
### References
- *Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR)*
- *Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)*
- *Data Subject Rights and Freedom*
per_question.ground_truth:
The GDPR grants supervisory authorities a wide range of corrective powers, including:

Warnings and reprimands

Orders to stop processing, correct, delete, or grant access to data

Suspension of international data transfers

Administrative fines

Reprimands are used for minor violations, while fines are imposed for serious breaches—especially when there’s risk of discrimination, identity theft, reputational harm, or misuse of sensitive or large-scale data. Fines can reach up to €20 million or 4% of global annual turnover, whichever is higher.

In addition to complaints to authorities, data subjects may seek compensation through court for material or non-material damages. Non-profit organizations can also file complaints or take legal action on behalf of data subjects.

per_question.input: When is a record of processing activities required under the GDPR and what does it contain?
per_question.output:
### **Records of Processing Activities Under the GDPR**

Under **Article 30 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR)**, controllers and processors are **required to maintain a record of processing activities** in specific circumstances. These requirements apply unless an exception is met (e.g., the organization employs fewer than 250 persons and the processing is not likely to result in a risk to data subjects' rights and freedoms, does not include special categories of data or criminal conviction data, and is not occasional). The obligation to maintain a record also applies regardless of the organization's size if the processing involves **special categories of data (e.g., health data, biometric data)** or **criminal conviction and offense data**, or if the processing is **not occasional** or **poses a risk to individuals' rights and freedoms**.

---

#### **Who Must Maintain a Record of Processing Activities?**

1. **Controllers** (or their representative):
   - Required to maintain a record of all **processing activities** under their responsibility.

2. **Processors** (or their representative):
   - Required to maintain a record of all **categories of processing activities** carried out on behalf of controllers.

---

#### **What Must the Records Contain?**

##### **For Controllers (Article 30(1)):**
The record must include the following information:

- The **name and contact details** of:
  - The controller
  - The controller's representative (if applicable)
  - The Data Protection Officer (DPO) (if designated)
- The **purposes of the processing**.

- A **description of the categories of data subjects** (e.g., employees, customers).

- A **description of the categories of personal data** being processed (e.g., names, email addresses, health data).

- A **list of recipients** (or categories of recipients) of the personal data, including whether they are in the EU or third countries.

- If applicable:
  - Transfers of personal data to **third countries or international organizations**, including:
    - Identification of the third country or international organization.
    - The existence or absence of an **adequacy decision** by the European Commission.
    - Reference to the **appropriate safeguards** (e.g., Standard Contractual Clauses) used and where to obtain a copy.
- The **envisaged time limits** for erasure of different categories of data (where possible).
- A **general description** of the **technical and organizational security measures** implemented to protect the data (referenced in Article 32).

---

##### **For Processors (Article 30(2)):**
The processor’s record must include:

- The **name and contact details** of:
  - The processor
  - Each controller on whose behalf the processor acts
  - The processor’s representative (if applicable)
  - The DPO (if designated)
- The **categories of processing activities** carried out on behalf of each controller.
- If applicable:
  - Transfers of personal data to **third countries or international organizations**, including:
    - Identification of the third country or organization.
    - Safeguards applied (e.g., adequacy decisions, binding corporate rules).
- A **general description** of the **technical and organizational security measures** implemented (referenced in Article 32).

---
##### **Additional Requirements:**
- The record must be maintained **in writing**, including in electronic form.
- It must be made **available to the supervisory authority upon request**.
- The record must be **kept up to date**.

---

### **References**

- [ * ] Regulation (EU) 2016/679 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 27 April 2016 on the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data, and repealing Directive 95/46/EC (General Data Protection Regulation)
- [ * ] GDPR Article 30 - Records of processing activities
per_question.ground_truth:
If members have 250 or more employees - keeping additional written records of all processing activities including:  o Name and details of organisation, and where applicable, other controllers, the member’s firm’s representative and data protection officer. o Purposes of the processing. o Description of the categories of individuals and personal data. o Categories of recipients to whom the personal data has been or will be disclosed. o Details of transfers to third countries (i.e. outside the EU) including the safeguards in place. o Retention schedules (where possible) 

There is a limited exemption if members have fewer than 250 employees.  Detailed records of processing activities only have to be kept where one of the following applies: o  higher risk processing, such as processing personal data which could result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of an individual,  o processing which is not occasional o processing of special categories of data (including that revealing race or ethnic origin, religious beliefs, political opinions, health data or genetic / biometric data) or criminal convictions and offences.

per_question.input: What is the primary objective of the AI Act?
per_question.output:
The primary objective of the **AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689)** is to **improve the functioning of the internal market** by establishing a **uniform legal framework** for the development, placing on the market, and use of **AI systems** in the Union. This framework aims to **promote trustworthy and human-centric artificial intelligence (AI)** while ensuring a **high level of protection** for health, safety, fundamental rights, democracy, the rule of law, and environmental protection—all in accordance with the **Charter of Fundamental Rights of the European Union**.

Key objectives include:
- **Preventing harmful effects of AI systems** in the Union by setting harmonized rules.
- **Ensuring the free movement of AI-based goods and services** across the internal market, while preventing Member States from imposing unjustified restrictions.
- **Balancing innovation with fundamental rights protection**, fostering a competitive environment for AI development and deployment.
- **Promoting AI literacy** to equip providers, deployers, and affected persons with the necessary knowledge and understanding to ensure **compliance and correct enforcement** of the regulation.

The AI Act also seeks to **prevent fragmentation of the internal market**, which could arise from divergent national rules, and to **support innovation** while upholding ethical and legal safeguards.

---
### References

- [5] Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amending Regulations (EC) No 300/2008, (EU) No 167/2013, (EU) No 168/2013, (EU) 2018/858, (EU) 2018/1139 and (EU) 2019/2144 and Directives 2014/90/EU, (EU) 2016/797 and (EU) 2020/1828 (Artificial Intelligence Act)

per_question.input: What approach does the AI Act propose for regulating AI systems?
per_question.output:
The **Artificial Intelligence Act (AI Act)**, officially known as **Regulation (EU) 2024/1689**, proposes a **risk-based approach** to regulate AI systems. This approach tailors the intensity and content of regulatory obligations to the level of risk posed by different types of AI systems, ensuring a proportionate and effective framework. Below are the key elements of this approach:

---

### **Core Principles of the Risk-Based Approach**

1. **Categorization by Risk Level**
   - AI systems are classified into **four risk categories** based on their potential impact on health, safety, fundamental rights, and public interests:
     - **Unacceptable Risk**: Prohibited practices that violate EU values or cause significant harm (e.g., social scoring, manipulative techniques, or systems exploiting vulnerabilities).
     - **High Risk**: AI systems that pose significant risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights. These are subject to stringent obligations.
     - **Limited or Low Risk**: AI systems with minimal risks (e.g., chatbots or deepfake tools), subject to transparency and informational requirements.
     - **Minimal Risk**: AI systems that present negligible risks (e.g., AI-enabled video games) and are largely unregulated, though voluntary codes of conduct may apply.

2. **Focus on High-Risk AI Systems**
   - **High-risk AI systems** are those used in sensitive areas such as:
     - Law enforcement, migration, and border control.
     - Justice and democratic processes.
     - Critical infrastructure (e.g., energy, transport, healthcare).
     - Employment (e.g., recruitment, monitoring).
     - Biometric identification and categorization (e.g., facial recognition).
     - Safety components of regulated products (e.g., machinery, medical devices).
   - These systems are subject to strict obligations, including:
     - Risk management systems.
     - High standards for data governance and quality.
     - Bias mitigation and transparency.
     - Comprehensive technical documentation.
     - Logging requirements.
     - Human oversight and post-market monitoring.
     - Conformity assessments and CE marking.
     - Registration in the EU database for high-risk systems listed in **Annex III**.
   - Providers must also conduct real-world testing and provide clear instructions for proper use.

3. **Obligations for Providers and Deployers**
   - **Providers** (entities that develop or place AI systems on the market) are responsible for:
     - Ensuring compliance with the AI Act.
     - Registering high-risk systems and their providers in the **EU database**.
     - Implementing risk management and post-market monitoring systems.
     - Providing technical documentation and ensuring AI systems are used as intended.
   - **Deployers** (entities that use AI systems) must:
     - Operate high-risk AI systems strictly according to provided instructions.
     - Ensure continuous monitoring of system performance during deployment.
     - Provide updated information about the AI systems they use.

4. **Transparency and Explanatory Requirements**
   - Certain AI systems (e.g., deepfakes, emotion recognition) must be accompanied by clear transparency information to enable individuals to make informed decisions.
   - High-risk AI systems must provide explanations for their decisions if used as the primary basis for significant actions affecting individuals.

5. **Regulatory Sandboxes**
   - The AI Act supports innovation by allowing AI systems to be developed and tested in **AI regulatory sandboxes**.
   - These sandboxes provide controlled environments for real-world testing under regulatory oversight, particularly for innovative AI systems.
   - SMEs and start-ups are granted priority access to these sandboxes.

6. **Prohibited AI Practices**
   - The AI Act explicitly prohibits certain AI practices, including:
     - AI systems that deploy **subliminal or manipulative techniques** to distort behavior.
     - Exploiting vulnerabilities of individuals (e.g., based on age, disability, or social circumstances).
     - Social scoring systems that unduly influence individuals.
     - AI systems for **real-time remote biometric identification** in public spaces (with limited exceptions for law enforcement).

7. **Harmonized Standards and Compliance**
   - Providers can rely on **harmonized standards** or **codes of practice** to demonstrate compliance with the AI Act.
   - The **European Commission** is empowered to assess compliance and issue common specifications for AI systems without applicable standards.

8. **Governance and Enforcement**
   - The AI Act establishes:
     - A **European AI Board** for consistent implementation across Member States.
     - A **Scientific Panel** to provide technical expertise and assess systemic risks.
     - **Market surveillance authorities** to monitor compliance and address violations.
     - **Cooperation between the Commission, Member States, and the AI Office** to ensure enforcement.
   - Penalties, including administrative fines, are imposed for non-compliance.

9. **International Alignment**
   - The AI Act aligns its definitions and regulatory approach with international organizations and standards to foster global consistency and facilitate trade.

---

### **Key Considerations**
- The risk-based approach ensures that **innovation is not stifled** while prioritizing the protection of fundamental rights and public interests.
- The AI Act applies to **all AI systems** placed on the market or used in the EU, regardless of where they are developed.
- The regulatory framework is designed to **balance technological advancement with safety, transparency, and ethical considerations**.

---

### References

- [3] Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council
- [5] Recital (6) and (26) of the AI Act
- [6] Recital (7) and (27) of the AI Act
- [9] Recital (65) and Article 9 of the AI Act
- [30] Recital (50) and (51) of the AI Act

per_question.input: What are the prohibited AI practices under the AI Act?
per_question.output:
The **Artificial Intelligence Act (AI Act)**—formally known as **Regulation (EU) 2024/1689**—establishes a **risk-based framework** for AI systems, categorizing them as **high-risk**, **limited-risk**, or **unacceptable (prohibited)**. Prohibited AI practices are those deemed to pose **unacceptable risks** to health, safety, fundamental rights, democracy, or the rule of law. These practices are explicitly listed in **Article 5** of the AI Act and are **banned across the EU**.

---

### **Key Prohibited AI Practices Under the AI Act**

#### **1. Subliminal, Manipulative, or Deceptive Techniques**
- **Ban**: AI systems that deploy **subliminal techniques** (beyond a person’s consciousness) or **purposefully manipulative/deceptive techniques** are prohibited.
- **Objective/Effect**: These techniques **materially distort** a person’s or group’s behavior, impairing their ability to make informed decisions.
- **Examples**:
  - AI systems designed to **nudge individuals** into decisions they wouldn’t otherwise make (e.g., purchasing harmful products or services).
  - AI-enabled **dark patterns** in digital interfaces that exploit cognitive biases.
- **Exception**: This prohibition does **not** apply to **lawful medical treatments** (e.g., psychological therapy) or **legitimate commercial practices** (e.g., targeted advertising compliant with EU law).
  *Source: [3, 29]*

---

#### **2. Exploitation of Vulnerabilities**
- **Ban**: AI systems that **exploit vulnerabilities** of individuals or groups due to:
  - **Age** (e.g., targeting children or the elderly).
  - **Disability** (e.g., cognitive or physical impairments).
  - **Social/economic situation** (e.g., poverty, minority status).
- **Objective/Effect**: These systems **materially distort behavior**, leading to **significant harm** to the individuals or groups targeted.
- **Key Point**: No intent to harm is required—**harm resulting from exploitation is sufficient** for prohibition.
  *Source: [5, 16, 29]*

---

#### **3. Social Scoring (Evaluation/Classification of Natural Persons or Groups)**
- **Ban**: AI systems that:
  - **Evaluate or classify** individuals or groups **based on their social behavior** or inferred/predicted personality traits.
  - Assign a **social score** leading to:
    - **Detrimental or unfavorable treatment** in unrelated social contexts (e.g., access to housing, education, or public services).
    - **Unjustified or disproportionate consequences** compared to the gravity of the behavior.
- **Rationale**: Such systems **violate fundamental rights** (e.g., non-discrimination, dignity) and enable **mass surveillance and exclusion**.
  *Source: [4, 31, 32]*

---
#### **4. Risk Assessments for Criminal Offenses (Based on Profiling or Personality Traits)**
- **Ban**: AI systems used to **assess or predict** the risk of a natural person committing a criminal offense **solely based on**:
  - **Profiling** (e.g., behavioral patterns, demographics).
  - **Personality traits or characteristics** (e.g., neuroticism, risk tolerance).
- **Exception**: Allowed **only if** the assessment **supports human decision-making** and is **based on objective, verifiable facts** linked to a criminal activity.
  *Source: [3, 42]*

---
#### **5. Untargeted Scraping for Facial Recognition Databases**
- **Ban**: AI systems that:
  - **Create or expand facial recognition databases** through **untargeted scraping** (e.g., collecting facial images from the internet or CCTV footage **without consent or clear purpose**).
- **Rationale**: This practice **compromises privacy**, fuels **surveillance fears**, and violates **data protection laws**.
  *Source: [3, 43]*

---
#### **6. Emotion Recognition in Workplace/ Education (Except for Medical/Safety Reasons)**
- **Ban**: AI systems used to **infer emotions** of individuals in:
  - **Workplace settings** (e.g., monitoring employee satisfaction or stress levels).
  - **Educational institutions** (e.g., assessing student engagement).
- **Exception**: Allowed **only for medical or safety reasons** (e.g., therapeutic use in mental health treatment).
- **Rationale**: Emotion recognition lacks scientific reliability, risks **discrimination**, and creates **power imbalances** in hierarchical environments.
  *Source: [3, 44]*

---
#### **7. Biometric Categorization (Deduction of Sensitive Attributes)**
- **Ban**: AI systems that **categorize individuals** based on biometric data to infer:
  - **Race or ethnicity**.
  - **Political opinions**.
  - **Religious or philosophical beliefs**.
  - **Trade union membership**.
  - **Sex life or sexual orientation**.
- **Exception**: Permissible if the categorization is **for law enforcement** or involves **lawful labeling/filtering of biometric datasets** (e.g., sorting images by hair color).
  *Source: [3, 30, g]*

---
#### **8. Real-Time Remote Biometric Identification in Public Spaces (Law Enforcement Use)**
- **Ban**: **"Real-time" remote biometric identification systems** (e.g., facial recognition used in **public spaces**) for **law enforcement purposes**, except under **strictly limited conditions**:
  1. **Search for specific victims** (e.g., abduction, human trafficking, or missing persons).
  2. **Prevention of imminent threats** (e.g., terrorism or serious physical harm).
  3. **Locating/pursuing a suspect** for crimes punishable by **≥4 years imprisonment**.
- **Limitation**: Must be **temporarily and geographically constrained** (e.g., not continuous mass surveillance).
- **Rationale**: High risk of **biased results**, **discrimination**, and **eroding privacy**.
  *Source: [3, h, 32, 45]*

---
#### **9. Bias-Based Profiling in Law Enforcement**
- **Ban**: AI systems used by law enforcement to:
  - **Assess the likelihood of offending** based **solely on profiling or personality traits** (e.g., predicting recidivism without objective evidence).
- **Rationale**: Reinforces **systemic biases** (e.g., racial, gender-based) and infringes on the **presumption of innocence**.
  *Source: [5, 42]*

---

### **Enforcement and Penalties**
- **Administrative Fines**: Violations can result in fines of up to **€1,500,000** or **4% of global turnover**, whichever is higher.
- **Market Restrictions**: Prohibited AI systems **cannot be placed on the market** or used in the EU.
- **Transparency Obligations**: Providers/deployers must ensure compliance and report violations.
  *Source: [Article 5, 2, 3, 4]*

---
### **Summary Table of Prohibited Practices**
| **Prohibited Practice**                          | **Key Rationale**                          | **Exception**                          |
|---------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Subliminal/Manipulative techniques                | Undermines informed decision-making        | Lawful medical treatments/commercial practices |
| Exploitation of vulnerabilities                   | Targets vulnerable groups                  | None                                   |
| Social scoring                                     | Leads to discrimination and exclusion      | None                                   |
| Criminal risk assessment (profiling/personality traits) | Violates presumption of innocence          | Human-supported assessments with factual evidence |
| Untargeted facial recognition scraping            | Enables mass surveillance                  | None                                   |
| Emotion recognition (workplace/education)        | Lacks reliability, discriminatory          | Medical/safety use only                |
| Biometric categorization (sensitive attributes)   | Violates privacy and dignity               | Law enforcement or lawful dataset tasks |
| Real-time remote biometric identification          | High risk of bias and erosion of privacy   | Strictly limited law enforcement use    |
| Bias-based profiling in law enforcement            | Reinforces systemic discrimination         | None                                   |

---
### **References**
- [1] Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence (AI Act) [Prohibited AI practices, Article 5]
- [2] Artificial Intelligence Act (AI Act) – Prohibited AI Practices [Explanation of manipulative and exploitative practices]
- [3] Artificial Intelligence Act – Prohibited Practices [Detailed provisions under Article 5]
- [4] Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 – Social Scoring and Discrimination [Impact on fundamental rights]
- [5] High-Risk AI Systems and Prohibited Practices [Classifications and enforcement]

per_question.input: How are high-risk AI systems defined in the AI Act?
per_question.output:
High-risk AI systems are specifically defined and regulated under the **AI Act (Regulation (EU) 2024/1689)** due to their potential to cause significant harm to health, safety, or fundamental rights. These systems are classified based on their intended purpose, the severity and probability of potential harm, and their compliance with strict obligations to mitigate risks. Below is a detailed breakdown of their definition and key characteristics:

---

### **1. Classification of High-Risk AI Systems**
High-risk AI systems are identified through two primary criteria under the AI Act:

#### **A. Systems Classified as High-Risk Based on Their Purpose**
These systems are explicitly listed in **Annex III** of the AI Act, which covers critical areas where accuracy, reliability, and transparency are essential to prevent adverse impacts. Examples include:
- **Law enforcement**: Systems intended for use by or on behalf of law enforcement authorities, such as:
  - AI systems used for assessing the risk of individuals becoming victims of criminal offenses.
  - Polygraphs and similar tools for evaluating the reliability of evidence during investigations.
  - Systems assessing the risk of individuals reoffending, unless prohibited under the AI Act.
  - AI tools used in administrative proceedings by tax and customs authorities or financial intelligence units for anti-money laundering purposes.
- **Migration, asylum, and border control**: Systems used for:
  - Assessing risks posed by individuals entering a Member State or applying for visas/asylum.
  - Assisting public authorities in examining asylum, visa, and residence permit applications.
  - Detecting, recognizing, or identifying natural persons in migration contexts (excluding travel document verification).
- **Administration of justice and democratic processes**: Systems used in:
  - Legal proceedings to assist judicial authorities.
  - Decision-making processes that could undermine democracy, the rule of law, or individual freedoms.
- **Biometric identification and categorization**: Systems used for:
  - Remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces (e.g., facial recognition).
  - Biometric categorization or emotion recognition.
- **Critical infrastructure**: Systems acting as safety components in sectors such as:
  - Energy, transport, and healthcare.
  - Products covered by **Union harmonisation legislation** (e.g., medical devices, vehicles, rail systems).
- **Education and vocational training**: Systems used for:
  - Evaluating or classifying natural persons for educational purposes (e.g., access to education or assessment of learning outcomes).
  - Biometric identification in educational institutions.
- **Employment and worker management**: Systems used for:
  - Recruitment, promotion, or retention of workers, particularly in contexts where historical biases may perpetuate discrimination.
  - Monitoring worker performance and behavior, which can infringe upon fundamental rights like privacy and non-discrimination.
- **Access to essential public services**: Systems used to determine whether individuals receive:
  - Social security benefits.
  - Healthcare services.
  - Housing assistance.
  - Or other essential public services.
- **Creditworthiness and financial services**: Systems used for:
  - Evaluating the credit score or creditworthiness of natural persons, impacting access to financial resources.
  - Risk assessment in life and health insurance.
- **Emergency services**: Systems used for:
  - Evaluating emergency calls.
  - Dispatching or prioritizing emergency services (e.g., police, firefighters, medical aid).
  - Triage in emergency healthcare settings.

**Exclusion from High-Risk Classification for Annex III Systems**:
AI systems listed in Annex III may **not** be considered high-risk if *all* of the following conditions are met:
1. The system is intended to perform a **narrow procedural task** (e.g., administrative processing).
2. The system is intended to **improve the result of a previously completed human activity** (e.g., proofreading a document for grammar or style).
3. The system is intended to **detect decision-making patterns** without replacing or influencing human assessment, provided there is proper human review.
4. The system is intended to perform a **preparatory task** for an assessment relevant to Annex III use cases (e.g., indexing or translating documents).

If these conditions are not met, the system remains classified as high-risk.

---

#### **B. Systems Classified as High-Risk Based on Union Harmonisation Legislation**
AI systems that act as **safety components** of products covered by specific Union harmonisation legislation (listed in **Annex I, Section A**) are automatically considered high-risk. These include:
- **Medical devices** (Regulation (EU) 2017/745).
- **In vitro diagnostic medical devices** (Regulation (EU) 2017/746).
- **Motor vehicles and their trailers** (Regulation (EU) 2019/2144, Regulation (EU) 2018/858).
- **Railway systems** (Directive (EU) 2016/797).
- **Civil aviation security equipment** (Regulation (EC) No 300/2008).
- **Agricultural and forestry vehicles** (Regulation (EU) No 167/2013).
- **Two- or three-wheel vehicles and quadricycles** (Regulation (EU) No 168/2013).
- **Marine equipment** (Directive 2014/90/EU).

These systems must undergo **third-party conformity assessments** to ensure compliance with safety and fundamental rights requirements before being placed on the market or put into service.

---

### **2. Key Obligations for High-Risk AI Systems**
High-risk AI systems are subject to stringent requirements to ensure safety, reliability, and accountability. These include:
- **Risk Management System**: Providers must implement a continuous, iterative risk management process throughout the AI system’s lifecycle. This involves:
  - Identifying and analyzing known and foreseeable risks.
  - Estimating and evaluating risks based on intended use and reasonably foreseeable misuse.
  - Adopting targeted risk mitigation measures.
- **Data Governance and Quality**: High-risk AI systems must be developed using high-quality training datasets that are relevant, representative, and free from biases.
- **Technical Documentation**: Comprehensive documentation must be maintained to demonstrate compliance with the AI Act, including:
  - Details of the AI system’s development, training, testing, and validation.
  - Risk assessment outcomes and mitigation strategies.
- **Transparency and Explainability**: High-risk AI systems must be designed to:
  - Provide clear instructions for use.
  - Ensure human oversight and the ability to interpret the system’s outputs.
  - Enable affected persons to request explanations for outcomes that significantly impact them.
- **Accuracy, Robustness, and Cybersecurity**: Systems must meet high standards for accuracy and robustness to prevent harmful behavior. Providers must also implement robust cybersecurity measures to protect against potential threats.
- **Conformity Assessment and Certification**: High-risk AI systems must undergo third-party conformity assessments by **notified bodies** to verify compliance. These assessments may include:
  - Evaluation of technical documentation.
  - Testing in real-world conditions.
  - Certification (e.g., CE marking).
- **Registration in the EU Database**: High-risk AI systems (including substantial modifications) must be registered in the **EU database**, which details their functionalities and use cases.
- **Post-Market Monitoring**: Providers must continuously monitor the performance of high-risk AI systems after they are placed on the market or put into service. This includes:
  - Investigating serious incidents or malfunctions.
  - Implementing corrective actions to address identified risks.
- **Fundamental Rights Impact Assessments**: In some cases, providers may need to conduct assessments to evaluate potential risks to fundamental rights (e.g., privacy, non-discrimination) and implement mitigation measures.

---
### **3. Exemptions and Derogations**
- **Public Security or Health Derogations**: In exceptional cases, market surveillance authorities may authorize the market entry of a high-risk AI system without prior conformity assessment if necessary for public security or health.
- **Free and Open-Source Licenses**: AI systems released under free and open-source licenses are not subject to the AI Act unless they are placed on the market or put into service as high-risk systems or fall under prohibited practices.
- **AI Regulatory Sandboxes**: Providers of high-risk AI systems may test their systems in real-world conditions within **AI regulatory sandboxes** under strict oversight.

---
### **4. Enforcement and Penalties**
Failure to comply with the obligations for high-risk AI systems may result in:
- **Restriction or Prohibition**: Authorities may restrict or prohibit the use of non-compliant AI systems.
- **Recall or Withdrawal**: Non-compliant systems may be withdrawn from the market.
- **Administrative Fines**: Providers or deployers may face fines proportional to the severity of the infringement.

---
### **5. Governance and Supervision**
- **Market Surveillance Authorities**: National authorities are responsible for monitoring and enforcing compliance with the AI Act.
- **AI Office**: The AI Office at the EU level supervises high-risk AI systems and ensures consistent enforcement across Member States.
- **Notified Bodies**: Independent bodies perform conformity assessments and issue certifications for high-risk AI systems.

---

### **References**
- [1] Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 13 June 2024 laying down harmonised rules on artificial intelligence and amending certain regulations and directives

per_question.input: What obligations are imposed on high-risk AI system providers?
per_question.output:
### **Obligations Imposed on Providers of High-Risk AI Systems**

Providers of **high-risk AI systems** bear significant legal and technical responsibilities to ensure compliance with the **Artificial Intelligence Act (EU Regulation 2024/1689)**. These obligations are designed to mitigate risks to health, safety, fundamental rights, and public interests. Below are the key obligations:

---

#### **1. Compliance with Core Requirements**
Providers must ensure that high-risk AI systems meet all applicable requirements, including:
- **Risk management system**: Establishing, implementing, and maintaining a continuous risk management process throughout the system’s lifecycle (Article 9).
- **Data governance and quality**: Ensuring high-quality training, validation, and testing datasets to minimize risks of bias and discrimination (Article 10).
- **Technical documentation**: Maintaining comprehensive documentation that demonstrates compliance with regulatory requirements (Article 11 and Annex IV).
- **Accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity**: Designing systems to achieve appropriate levels of performance and resilience against cyber threats (Article 15).
- **Human oversight**: Implementing measures to ensure adequate human control over the system (Article 14).
- **Transparency and information**: Providing clear instructions for use, technical specifications, and CE marking (Article 16).

---

#### **2. Quality Management System (QMS)**
Providers must establish a **quality management system** that ensures compliance with regulatory requirements. This system should include:
- Processes for risk identification, mitigation, and post-market monitoring.
- Procedures for handling incidents, complaints, and corrective actions (Article 17).
- Regular audits and reviews to maintain compliance (Article 43).

---

#### **3. Conformity Assessment and Certification**
Providers must:
- Conduct a **conformity assessment** to verify that the system meets all regulatory requirements (Article 43).
- Issue an **EU declaration of conformity** and affix the **CE marking** before placing the system on the market (Articles 47 and 48).
- Register the system in the **EU database** and notify relevant authorities (Article 49).

---

#### **4. Post-Market Monitoring and Incident Reporting**
Providers are required to:
- Establish a **post-market monitoring system** to track performance and identify risks after deployment (Article 72).
- Immediately notify **competent authorities** of any **serious incidents** or malfunctions (Article 73).
- Report systemic risks or widespread infringements to the **AI Office** or **Commission** (Article 73).

---

#### **5. Transparency and Accountability Measures**
Providers must:
- Ensure **traceability** of the system’s outputs and decisions (Article 19).
- Provide **detailed instructions for use** to deployers, including limitations and expected performance (Article 16).
- Implement measures to detect and mitigate **foreseeable misuse** (Article 16).
- Enable **technical access** for conformity assessments and market surveillance (Article 16).

---

#### **6. Accessibility and Ethical Considerations**
Providers must ensure that high-risk AI systems comply with **accessibility requirements** (e.g., Directive (EU) 2019/882) and do not lead to discriminatory outcomes. They must also consider the impact on **vulnerable groups** and mitigate risks of harm to fundamental rights (Article 16).

---

#### **7. Cooperation with Competent Authorities**
Providers must:
- Cooperate with **market surveillance authorities** and provide requested documentation or access to the system or its logs.
- Respond promptly to requests for information or corrective actions (Article 16).

---
#### **8. Specific Obligations for High-Risk AI Systems in Annex III**
Providers of systems listed in **Annex III** (e.g., those used in law enforcement, migration, or justice) must:
- Ensure **independent verification** by at least two competent persons, unless exempt under Article 13(2).
- Meet additional requirements such as **fundamental rights impact assessments** and **real-world testing** for certain use cases.

---

#### **9. Liability and Legal Compliance**
Providers remain liable for damages caused by their systems under **Union and national liability laws**. Non-compliance may result in:
- **Administrative fines** of up to **3% of global annual turnover** (or **€15 million**, whichever is higher) (Article 99).
- **Corrective actions**, including recalls or market withdrawals.

---

#### **10. Additional Responsibilities Under Union Harmonisation Legislation**
For high-risk AI systems that are also **safety components of products** (e.g., medical devices), providers must ensure compliance with **Union harmonisation legislation** (e.g., Regulation (EU) 2017/745). If the AI system is integrated into a product, the **product manufacturer** may assume the provider’s obligations (Article 25).

---
### **Key References Supporting This Response**
Providers must ensure their high-risk AI systems are compliant with all requirements and undergo conformity assessments. They must maintain technical documentation, implement risk management systems, and cooperate with authorities to ensure ongoing compliance and transparency.

---

### References

- [1] Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (Article 16, 17, 43, 49, 72, 73)
- [2] Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (Annex IV, Article 9, 10, 11)
- [3] Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (Article 47, 48, 99)
- [4] Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (Article 19, 14)
